articles.title: Courts Are Rewriting the Rules of the 2026 Midterms
articles.summary: A wave of rulings and lawsuits over voter data, mail ballots, and congressional maps is forcing states, courts, and the White House into a fight over who really controls the next election.

articles.title: How the 2026 box-office rebound is testing Hollywood’s streaming bargain
articles.summary: A stronger spring at the movies is reviving old promises about theatrical culture, but the business and labor math still looks fragile.

articles.title: Inside the new bottleneck shaping AI: power, permits, and chips
articles.summary: The AI boom is no longer just a software story. It is colliding with utilities, grid queues, chip export rules, and local politics—and that is reshaping who gets to build the next generation of digital infrastructure.

articles.title: Tariffs are back in the inflation data—and in the family budget
articles.summary: March’s price spike, fresh Fed analysis and new IMF warnings point to a household squeeze that is showing up first in gas, goods and expectations, with rent and wage gains offering only partial relief.

articles.title: Workforce Pell Is About to Land. Colleges and students are racing to prepare.
articles.summary: A new federal aid experiment could reshape short-term training, but weak oversight, thin state data and a strained aid system raise questions about who benefits and who gets left out.

articles.title: U.S. measles surge enters late-spring danger zone as outbreaks spread, vaccination gaps widen
articles.summary: With more than 1,700 U.S. cases already this year and outbreaks spreading across multiple states, public-health officials are racing to keep measles from exploiting falling MMR coverage, travel-linked introductions and under-vaccinated pockets.

articles.title: The New Intimacy Machine
articles.summary: AI companions promise to ease loneliness, but they also monetize attachment, blur consent, and shift care from public life into private extraction.

articles.title: As federal science archives recede, researchers race to save the public record
articles.summary: Across climate, health and basic research, scientists and librarians are building parallel archives as federal data portals, webpages and tooling become harder to rely on.

articles.title: Sudan’s border war: how a three-year conflict is remaking Chad, aid routes, and regional security
articles.summary: As Sudan’s war enters its fourth year, the fighting is no longer contained by the country’s frontiers: it is redrawing the humanitarian map in Chad, hardening border politics, and exposing how dependent relief still is on fragile regional corridors.

articles.title: Trump’s tariff machine is no longer trade policy. Congress should say so.
articles.summary: The president’s 2026 tariff escalation has turned a once-disputed import tax into a standing power to reprice the economy. The right answer is not another court fight by proxy. It is a statute that restores Congress’s tariff authority, limits emergency levies, and forces a real industrial policy debate into the open.

articles.title: AI’s next regulatory clash is arriving in classrooms, agencies and chip racks at once
articles.summary: As the EU AI Act’s main rules move into force, governments and schools are shifting from broad AI principles to enforcement, procurement and training — while companies race to sell safer systems, and critics warn the fixes may lag the technology.

articles.title: U.S.-Iran brinkmanship turns the Strait of Hormuz into the world’s most expensive chokepoint again
articles.summary: With talks sputtering, sanctions tightening and the Strait of Hormuz under pressure, the crisis is testing deterrence, allied coordination and the resilience of global trade routes.

articles.title: Hollywood’s Window Wars Enter a New Phase as Studios Reopen the Theater Question
articles.summary: At CinemaCon and in earnings calls, the industry is signaling a partial return to theatrical-first strategy — but the new consensus may be narrower, more premium, and more conditional than exhibitors want.

articles.title: Coachella 2026 Is Rewriting Pop’s Power Map in Real Time
articles.summary: From Sabrina Carpenter’s coronation to Justin Bieber’s viral return and the platform logic underneath both, this year’s Coachella is less a festival than a live stress test for how fame, fandom, and streaming now work.

articles.title: How urban beekeeping became a climate hobby, a data network, and a politics of the sidewalk
articles.summary: From rooftop apiaries to youth fellowships and city pollinator plans, urban beekeeping is being remade as a test case for ecology, education, and neighborhood legitimacy.
